region_of_origin: Global; served in Singapore Chinese cuisine context

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301487 covers oyster as a food item; no specific raw/chilled preparation subclass exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw oyster served chilled on the half shell, approximately 50 g edible flesh, as typically served at a Singapore seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, eastern, wild, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw eastern oyster used as the closest approved reference for a raw/chilled oyster served in a Singapore Chinese seafood context. Pacific oyster (Crassostrea gigas) nutrient profile is closely comparable to eastern oyster (Crassostrea virginica) for core macros and micronutrients; values are representative. No Singapore HPB Foodo entry for plain raw oyster was identified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw oyster exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Oysters are a low-carbohydrate shellfish (3.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly glycogen). Shellfish and seafood are consistently classified as very low GI foods (GI < 15) in peer-reviewed literature. A GI of 5 is assigned as a conservative low estimate consistent with the glycogen-based carbohydrate content of bivalves, analogous to other low-carbohydrate seafood entries in the Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (5 × 1.95) / 100 ≈ 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (211 mg per 100 g); single oyster contributes ~106 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of cumulative sodium intake when consuming multiple oysters, particularly if served with high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce, mignonette, or hot sauce; a single chilled oyster contributes approximately 106 mg sodium, which is moderate within a controlled daily sodium budget of 1500–2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~5) and low carbohydrate content (3.9 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw oysters are an excellent protein and micronutrient source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low glycemic index, minimal carbohydrate load, and high zinc content (39.3 mg per 100 g), which supports insulin signalling; portion size should be monitored if served with high-sugar or high-sodium dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid profile and low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw oysters provide a cardioprotective nutrient profile with low saturated fat, meaningful omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (0.9 g per 100 g), and high selenium (63.7 mcg per 100 g), which supports antioxidant defence; cholesterol content (55 mg per 100 g) is moderate and unlikely to be clinically significant in the context of a balanced diet for most cardiovascular patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw chilled oyster (冰镇生蚝) is a whole bivalve mollusc served fresh on the half shell over crushed ice, as offered at Singapore Chinese seafood restaurants such as Hai Kah Lang. The oyster is consumed raw without cooking, preserving its natural briny, oceanic flavour and soft, creamy, slightly slippery texture. In the Singapore Chinese dining context, chilled raw oysters are typically served as a premium appetiser or seafood platter item, often accompanied by lemon wedges, mignonette sauce, or light soy-based dipping sauces. Nutritionally, raw oysters are a low-calorie, high-protein shellfish providing approximately 68 kcal, 7.1 g protein, and only 2.5 g fat per 100 g. They are exceptionally rich in zinc (39.3 mg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (8.75 mcg per 100 g), selenium (63.7 mcg per 100 g), and iron (5.1 mg per 100 g), making them one of the most micronutrient-dense foods available. Their very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~5) and minimal carbohydrate content (3.9 g per 100 g, primarily glycogen) make them highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The moderate sodium content (211 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension, especially when paired with salty condiments. Oysters are a shellfish allergen and are not halal-certified in most Singapore contexts. They are gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in one oyster?
What nutrients are in raw oyster?
Is oyster good for heart health?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for plain raw/chilled oyster was identified. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for raw eastern oyster (FDC ID 175180), which is the closest approved reference. Pacific oyster and eastern oyster have closely comparable nutritional profiles for core macros and key micronutrients. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed shellfish GI literature. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw eastern oyster (FDC ID 175180); Pacific oyster values are comparable. 2. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for raw oyster exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per shellfish literature consensus. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source (naturally zero for raw shellfish). 4. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; oyster carbohydrate is primarily glycogen, not free sugars. 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero for animal-origin shellfish with no plant fibre. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, all B vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FDC ID 175180. 7. Halal status is false as oysters are generally not certified halal in Singapore seafood contexts. 8. The input food name '冰镇生蚝 Oyster' from GrabFood Singapore (Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall) has been preserved in aliases. 9. Serving size estimated at 50 g per single oyster piece consistent with typical medium Pacific oyster edible flesh weight.
